abstract.t: This document defines the Web Content Authenticity Protocol (WCAP), a mechanism for ensuring the end-to-end authenticity and integrity of web resources. WCAP is designed to be transport-agnostic, meaning it can verify content regardless of how it was delivered — via HTTP, peer-to-peer networks, or other channels. By using digital signatures, WCAP allows a verifier to cryptographically confirm that a resource is exactly what the originator intended to publish, and has not been tampered with by any intermediary, including compromised servers, state-level actors, or malicious networks. Version 0.3 extends the protocol with an optional Signing Policy Declaration mechanism, allowing Signers to declare what they verified about content provenance and integrity before signing, enabling verifiers to distinguish transport-integrity-only signatures from signatures backed by deeper content verification.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: While Transport Layer Security (TLS) provides a secure channel between a client and a server, it does not protect content once it has been delivered or if an intermediary in the chain (such as a CDN or proxy) is compromised. The Web Content Authenticity Protocol (WCAP) provides a mechanism for a web server (the "Signer") to digitally sign web resources, allowing a client (the "Verifier") to confirm that the content is exactly what the origin server intended to send.

The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Threat Model
section.t: WCAP is designed to protect against the following threats:

dl.dd: A malicious actor alters content in transit.
A CDN, reverse proxy, or hosting server is compromised.
An adversary reroutes traffic to a malicious server.
A rogue or coerced CA enables a man-in-the-middle attack.
A government forces a platform to alter or remove content.
A publisher is forced to hand over their private signing keys.

A Signer forwards content from an untrusted or unverified upstream source without disclosing this to the Verifier, causing the Verifier to incorrectly infer that the Signer vouches for the content's origin. The Signing Policy mechanism (Section 13) addresses this threat by making the Signer's verification posture explicit.
Any HTTP-delivered content (HTML, JavaScript, images, JSON, etc.).
The server or entity that holds a private key and signs Resources.
The client or tool that retrieves and verifies the Signature using the Signer's public key.
The result of signing a Resource body with a private key.
A URI where the Signer's public key is published.

A declared assurance level indicating what the Signer verified about the content before signing. Expressed as a registered token in the "policy" parameter of the Content-Signature header (Section 13).

section.name: Example: P-256 (Default Suite)
section.t: Step 1: Generate a Key Pair
section.artwork: openssl ecparam -name prime256v1 -genkey -noout -out private-key.pem openssl ec -in private-key.pem -pubout -out public-key.pem
section.t: Step 2: Sign Your Content
section.artwork: openssl dgst -sha256 -sign private-key.pem -out signature.bin message.txt openssl base64 -in signature.bin -out signature.b64
section.t: Step 3: Serve Content with Content-Signature Header
Ensure the public key is served at the path specified in key_uri.

section.name: Example: DD-ECIES (secp256k1 Suite)
section.t: This example demonstrates signing content using the "dd-ecies-secp256k1-sha256" algorithm suite. The signature uses ECDSA over secp256k1 with SHA-256, producing a 64-byte compact signature as defined in the DD-ECIES specification .
Step 1: Generate a secp256k1 Key Pair
section.artwork: openssl ecparam -name secp256k1 -genkey -noout \ -out private-key-secp256k1.pem openssl ec -in private-key-secp256k1.pem -pubout \ -conv_form compressed -out public-key-secp256k1.pem
section.t: The public key MUST be a 33-byte compressed secp256k1 key (0x02 or 0x03 prefix byte) encoded in PEM format.
Step 2: Sign Your Content

Compute the SHA-256 hash of the content, then sign the hash using ECDSA with deterministic nonce generation per . The resulting signature MUST be a 64-byte compact format: r (32 bytes) || s (32 bytes), with no DER encoding. A DD-ECIES-compatible tool or library is required to produce the compact signature format.
Step 3: Serve Content with Content-Signature Header

section.name: Protocol Flow

ol.li: The Signer generates the Resource body and its Signature.
The Signer returns the Resource with the Content-Signature HTTP header.
The Verifier retrieves the public key from key_uri.
The Verifier checks the Signature against the Resource body.

section.t: If verification succeeds, the Resource is authentic. If it fails, the Resource is potentially tampered with.

ul.li: Signers MUST generate and securely store a public/private key pair.
RECOMMENDED algorithm: ECDSA (P-256 with SHA-256).
Signers MAY support other modern, secure digital signature algorithms.
Signers SHOULD rotate keys periodically and expose metadata for revocation and validity.
Signers MUST publish their public key at a stable key_uri, such as "/.well-known/wcap-public-key.pem".
The key MUST be served over HTTPS with a valid certificate.
The public key MUST be in PEM format.
Signers MAY include a revocation URI and expiration metadata.

section.name: The Content-Signature Header
section.t: Header format:
section.artwork: Content-Signature: alg=<algorithm>; key_uri=<uri>; sig=<base64 signature>[; kid=<key-id>][; policy=<policy-token>]

dl.dd: REQUIRED. Algorithm suite identifier. MUST be a registered identifier from the Algorithm Suite Registry (). Valid values include "ecdsa-p256-sha256" (default) and "dd-ecies-secp256k1-sha256".
REQUIRED. Public key location (relative or absolute URI). The key served at this URI MUST be in PEM format and MUST conform to the key format specified by the algorithm suite.
REQUIRED. Base64-encoded ( Section 4) digital signature of the exact response body. The signature encoding and size MUST conform to the signature format specified by the algorithm suite.

OPTIONAL. Key ID string identifying which key the signer used. This parameter supports signers that publish multiple keys (e.g., for different algorithm suites or key rotation). When present, verifiers SHOULD use the kid value to select the appropriate public key for verification.

OPTIONAL. Signing policy token declaring what the Signer verified about the content before signing. MUST be a registered token from the Signing Policy Registry (). When absent, verifiers MUST treat the signature as making no claim beyond transport integrity.

section.name: Algorithm-Specific Requirements
section.t: When alg=ecdsa-p256-sha256:

ul.li: The "sig" field MUST contain a base64-encoded DER-encoded ECDSA signature.
The public key served at key_uri MUST be an uncompressed P-256 public key in PEM format.

section.t: When alg=dd-ecies-secp256k1-sha256:

ul.li: The "sig" field MUST contain a base64-encoded 64-byte compact ECDSA signature in the format r(32 bytes) || s(32 bytes), as defined in the DD-ECIES specification .
The public key served at key_uri MUST be a 33-byte compressed secp256k1 public key (0x02 or 0x03 prefix byte) encoded in PEM format.

section.name: Signature Discovery
section.t: Verifiers MUST check for a Content-Signature header in HTTP responses.

ul.li: Verifiers MUST fetch the public key from key_uri (HTTPS RECOMMENDED).
Verifiers SHOULD cache keys securely for performance.
Verifiers MAY allow preloading or pinning of trusted keys.

section.name: Verification Logic
section.t: The Verifier MUST:

ol.li: Retrieve and cache the public key.
Verify the decoded "sig" against the exact body of the response.

ul.li: Verifiers MUST NOT render or use the resource.
Verifiers SHOULD display a clear integrity warning or error.

section.name: Static Assets (JavaScript, CSS)
section.t: Implementations SHOULD continue to use Subresource Integrity (SRI) when possible. WCAP MAY be used as a fallback or enhancement to SRI.

section.name: WebSockets
section.t: To sign WebSocket messages, the signature is included inline:
Every message MUST be signed individually. The receiver MUST verify before acting on the message.

dl.dd: Signers MUST allow for key rotation and revocation.
Resources MAY include timestamps or nonces.
WCAP relies on HTTPS or DNSSEC for the first key fetch.
Clients SHOULD treat unsigned content as untrusted when a signature is expected.
Future versions MAY support binding the signature to additional headers (e.g., MIME types, CSP).

section.name: Privacy Considerations
section.t: Public key fetches can expose access patterns. To reduce this:

ul.li: Verifiers SHOULD cache keys aggressively.
Verifiers MAY pre-fetch public keys for known hosts.
Alternative key distribution (e.g., IPFS, blockchain, DNSSEC) MAY enhance privacy and resilience.
Verifiable streaming content using Merkle trees.
Multi-signature schemes or endorsement chains.
Web browser integration (e.g., extensions or native WCAP support).
Blockchain-based or decentralized key registries.
Cryptographic proof of signing policy compliance (e.g., zero-knowledge proofs that decryption succeeded without revealing key material).

section.name: Overview
section.t: A WCAP signature cryptographically proves that the Signer held the private key and that the signed bytes were not altered after signing. It does not, by itself, say anything about what the Signer verified about the content before signing.

The "policy" parameter in the Content-Signature header allows a Signer to declare, as part of the signed response, what it verified before signing. The declaration is a registered token from the Signing Policy Registry defined in this section.

Important limitation: A signing policy declaration is a self-asserted claim by the Signer. It is not cryptographically provable by the Verifier. Verifiers that rely on policy declarations MUST establish trust in the Signer's honesty through out-of-band means (e.g., audits, published signing policy documentation, or reputation). The "policy" parameter is a transparency mechanism, not a proof mechanism.

section.name: Registry Structure
section.t: Each entry in the Signing Policy Registry MUST include: Token (the value used as the "policy" parameter, consisting only of lowercase ASCII letters, digits, and hyphens), Name (a short human-readable name), Assurance Level (an integer from 0 to 3, where 0 means no claim and 3 is the strongest claim), Description (a normative statement of what the Signer MUST have verified), and Example Use Case (a non-normative example).

section.name: none (Assurance Level 0)
section.t: Token: none. The Signer makes no claim about what was verified before signing. The signature attests only to transport integrity. This is equivalent to omitting the "policy" parameter entirely. Example: A reverse proxy that signs all outbound responses without inspecting content.

section.name: content-verified (Assurance Level 1)
section.t: Token: content-verified. The Signer verified that the content was retrievable and well-formed before signing. The Signer MUST have successfully fetched or reconstructed the content from its authoritative source and confirmed that the retrieval did not produce an error. The Signer makes no claim about the content's origin or ownership. Example: A file server that reads from a local disk and confirms the file exists and is readable before signing.

section.name: origin-verified (Assurance Level 2)
section.t: Token: origin-verified. The Signer verified that the content was retrieved from a known, trusted origin and that the retrieval was authenticated. The Signer MUST have confirmed the identity of the upstream source (e.g., via a verified signature, authenticated storage credential, or cryptographic commitment) before signing. Example: A CDN edge node that fetches content from an origin server over a mutually authenticated TLS connection and verifies the origin's identity certificate before signing.

section.name: decryption-verified (Assurance Level 3)
section.t: Token: decryption-verified. The Signer retrieved the content from encrypted storage, successfully decrypted it using the appropriate key material, and is signing the plaintext result. Successful decryption constitutes cryptographic proof of content integrity and authenticity: a ciphertext that decrypts without error under the correct key was produced by an entity that held that key and has not been tampered with since encryption. The Signer MUST NOT set this policy if decryption produced an error or if the content was served from a cache without re-verifying the ciphertext. Example: A file-serving system that stores files as ECIES-encrypted blocks. Before signing the response, the server decrypts the block using the member's key material. A successful decryption guarantees the block was encrypted by a legitimate participant and has not been corrupted or substituted.

section.name: Signer Requirements
section.t: When a Signer includes a "policy" parameter:

ol.li: The Signer MUST have performed all verifications described in the policy's normative description before signing.
The Signer MUST NOT assert a policy token for verifications it did not perform.
The Signer SHOULD document its signing policy publicly (e.g., in a "/.well-known/wcap-signing-policy.json" resource).
If the Signer cannot complete the required verifications, it MUST either omit the "policy" parameter, use a lower-assurance token, or omit the Content-Signature header entirely.

section.name: Verifier Requirements
section.t: When a Verifier processes a Content-Signature header containing a "policy" parameter:

ol.li: The Verifier MUST first verify the cryptographic signature as defined in Section 7.3. A valid signature is a prerequisite for trusting any policy claim.
The Verifier MAY enforce a minimum assurance level. If the declared policy's assurance level is below the required minimum, the Verifier SHOULD treat the response as if no policy was declared.
The Verifier MUST NOT treat a policy declaration as cryptographic proof of the claimed verifications.
When a "policy" parameter contains an unrecognized token, the Verifier SHOULD treat it as assurance level 0 and MAY log a diagnostic warning.

section.name: Example Header with Policy
section.t: This header declares that the content was retrieved from encrypted storage and successfully decrypted before signing.

section.name: Extensibility
section.t: New signing policy tokens MAY be registered by adding an entry with all required fields. Registration MUST NOT require modifications to the core protocol sections or the Algorithm Suite Registry. Policy tokens are independent of algorithm suites; any policy MAY be combined with any algorithm suite.

section.name: Overview
section.t: WCAP supports pluggable algorithm suites, analogous to TLS cipher suites. An algorithm suite is a named combination of cryptographic algorithms — including an elliptic curve, signature algorithm, hash function, key format, and signature format — that signers and verifiers use to produce and verify content signatures.

The "alg" parameter in the Content-Signature header is the sole mechanism for algorithm negotiation. The core protocol logic MUST NOT contain algorithm-specific behavior; all algorithm-dependent details are encapsulated within the suite definition.

section.name: Registry Structure
section.t: Each algorithm suite entry MUST include:

dl.dd: The token used as the "alg" parameter value.
The elliptic curve used for key generation.
The digital signature algorithm.
The hash function used for message digests.
The encoding and size of the public key served at key_uri.
The encoding and size of the signature in the "sig" parameter.
The defining specification or standard.

section.name: ecdsa-p256-sha256 (Default)
section.t: Identifier: ecdsa-p256-sha256. Curve: P-256 (secp256r1) per . Signature Algorithm: ECDSA. Hash: SHA-256. Key Format: PEM-encoded uncompressed P-256 public key. Signature Format: DER-encoded ECDSA signature, base64. Reference: , .
This is the default suite. All conforming verifiers MUST support it.

section.name: dd-ecies-secp256k1-sha256
section.t: Identifier: dd-ecies-secp256k1-sha256. Curve: secp256k1 per section 2.4.1. Signature Algorithm: ECDSA with deterministic nonce generation per . Hash: SHA-256. Key Format: PEM-encoded 33-byte compressed secp256k1 public key (0x02/0x03 prefix). Signature Format: 64-byte compact signature (r || s, 32 bytes each), base64-encoded. Reference: .
Conforming verifiers SHOULD support this suite.

section.name: Algorithm Suite Negotiation
section.t: The "alg" parameter is the sole negotiation mechanism. The core protocol (Sections 5-7) MUST NOT contain algorithm-specific behavior. All algorithm-dependent logic MUST be delegated to the suite definition.

section.name: Handling Unrecognized Algorithm Suites
section.t: When a verifier encounters an unrecognized "alg" value:

ol.li: The verifier MUST reject the signature.
The verifier SHOULD report the unsupported algorithm.
The verifier MUST NOT attempt verification with a different suite.

section.name: Extensibility
section.t: New algorithm suites MAY be registered by adding an entry with all required fields. Registration MUST NOT require modifications to the core protocol sections.

ul.li: Verifiers MUST support "ecdsa-p256-sha256".
Verifiers SHOULD support "dd-ecies-secp256k1-sha256".
Verifiers MAY support additional registered suites.

front.title: SEC 2: Recommended Elliptic Curve Domain Parameters, Version 2.0
DD-ECIES: Digital Defiance Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme

author.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology
